message.content:
## Adversarial Claim Verifier (voter 1/3)

Be SKEPTICAL. Try to REFUTE this claim. ≥2/3 refutations kill it.

## Research question
Best algorithms/techniques to make audio the most comprehensible (e.g. time-scale modification like WSOLA/PSOLA, intelligibility enhancement, silence/pause compression, spectral and prosody processing), AND how "speed listening" works at playback speeds greater than 16x — the perceptual limits of accelerated speech comprehension, training/adaptation effects, and the signal-processing methods that make extreme speed-ups intelligible.

## Claim under review
"Reduced comprehension of time-compressed speech is primarily caused by decreased intelligibility of individual words, not just by the faster delivery rate per se."

**Source:** https://link.springer.com/article/10.3758/BF03208200 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Decreased comprehension of compressed messages is the result of decreased word intelligibility."

## Checklist
1. Is the claim actually supported by the quote, or is it an overreach/misread?
2. WebSearch for contradicting evidence — does any credible source dispute or heavily qualify this?
3. Is the source quality sufficient for the claim's strength? (extraordinary claims need primary sources)
4. Is the claim outdated? (check dates — old claims about fast-moving fields are suspect)
5. Is this a marketing claim / press release / cherry-picked benchmark / forum speculation?

**refuted=true** if: unsupported by quote / contradicted / low-quality source for strong claim / outdated / marketing fluff.
**refuted=false** ONLY if: claim is well-supported, current, and source quality matches claim strength.
Default to refuted=true if uncertain.

Structured output only. Evidence MUST be specific.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Based on the search results, here's what research shows about how time-compressed speech affects comprehension and word intelligibility:

## How Delivery Rate Affects Comprehension

Conversational speech in English takes place at around 150 words per minute (wpm), and people can comprehend speech at rates of 200-250 wpm without difficulty. However, comprehension decreases slowly up to 275 wpm but more rapidly beyond that point.

## The Cause of Decreased Word Intelligibility

When speech speed is increased, it raises the pitch of the voices, which can reduce intelligibility. Additionally, during fast speech production, fewer acoustic or articulatory targets are reached, which makes fast speech sound mumbled and less intelligible.

Decreased comprehension of compressed messages is the result of decreased word intelligibility. Research shows that individually compressed words are less intelligible when presented within the limited time constraints of a compressed message.

## The Role of Pauses

Another factor is that reducing pauses is possibly the strongest acoustic change when speaking faster. The presence of pauses helps maintain semantic and syntactic cues important for comprehension.

In summary, the primary cause of decreased word intelligibility in time-compressed speech is the combination of acoustic changes—such as increased pitch and reduced articulatory targets—along with reduced pauses and compressed timing constraints that make individual words harder to perceive.

Exploring time compression in simultaneous interpreting - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639315001041"},{"title":"(PDF) Perceptual learning of time-compressed and natural fast speech","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/38072250_Perceptual_learning_of_time-compressed_and_natural_fast_speech"},{"title":"Intelligibility of time-compressed synthetic speech: Compression method and speaking style | Request PDF","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/282424493_Intelligibility_of_time-compressed_synthetic_speech_Compression_method_and_speaking_style"},{"title":"Recognition of time-compressed speech does not predict recognition of natural fast-rate speech by older listeners - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6377055/"},{"title":"Natural fast speech is perceived as faster than linearly time-compressed speech | Attention, Perception, & Psychophysics | Springer Nature Link","url":"https://link.springer.com/article/10.3758/s13414-016-1067-x"},{"title":"Speech Rate and Syntactic Complexity as Multiplicative Factors in Speech Comprehension by Young and Older Adults | Request PDF","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/247491132_Speech_Rate_and_Syntactic_Complexity_as_Multiplicative_Factors_in_Speech_Comprehension_by_Young_and_Older_Adults"},{"title":"The relationship between the intelligibility of time-compressed speech and speech in noise in young and elderly listeners - PubMed","url":"https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11831813/?dopt=Abstract"}]

Based on the web search results, I found information about Janse's 2004 research on time-compressed speech comprehension:

## Key Findings from Janse (2004)

Natural fast speech is more difficult to process than speech that is artificially time compressed to the same rate. 

### Processing Time

Listeners demonstrated a longer processing time for natural fast-rate speech than for time-compressed speech, which was interpreted as reflecting the impact of less precise articulation in natural fast speech. The study measured this using a phoneme detection task where listeners pressed a button when they detected a specified word-initial phoneme.

### Intelligibility

Recognition accuracy for naturally produced fast speech is poorer than for time-compressed speech. The result was attributed to the reduction of juncture pauses and to the less careful articulation in fast natural speech.

### Study Methodology

The stimuli were multisyllabic nouns with a word-initial plosive consonant embedded in sentence fragments, with a fast-to-normal ratio of 0.72, representing a modest increment in speech rate.

The research demonstrates that linear time compression preserves intelligibility better than natural fast speech at equivalent rates, likely because linear compression maintains more consistent pause patterns and articulation characteristics than natural speech production.

content.content:
# Article Summary

**Title:** Word intelligibility decrements and the comprehension of time-compressed speech

**Authors:** Gary W. Heiman, Raphael J. Leo, Glenn Leighbody, and Kathleen Bowler

**Year:** 1986

**Abstract:** The researchers investigated whether reduced comprehension of time-compressed messages stems from diminished word intelligibility. They conducted two experiments using 500-word messages. The first involved temporal interruption of speech, while the second employed backward masking techniques.

## Key Conclusion

The study concludes that "decreased comprehension of compressed messages is the result of decreased word intelligibility" rather than the accelerated delivery rate itself. This finding suggests the causal mechanism involves difficulty perceiving individual words under time constraints, not simply faster speech per se.

## Experimental Details

**Compression rates tested:** The study examined conditions where "60% of the signal is deleted," demonstrating comprehension reduction even without temporal compression constraints.

**Experimental design:** 
- Experiment 1: Temporally interrupted 500-word messages
- Experiment 2: Backward masking procedure applied to individually compressed words presented within compressed message constraints

The research demonstrates that word-level perceptual degradation, rather than speech rate acceleration alone, drives comprehension deficits.

Web search results for query: "comprehension of compressed speech limited by central processing time not peripheral intelligibility rate"

Links: [{"title":"The Intelligibility of Time-Compressed Speech Is Correlated with the Ability to Listen in Modulated Noise - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9085996/"},{"title":"The importance of temporal-fine structure to perceive time-compressed speech with and without the restoration of the syllabic rhythm | Scientific Reports","url":"https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-023-29755-x"},{"title":"A Temporal Bottleneck in the Language Comprehension Network - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6622343/"},{"title":"The Intelligibility of Time-Compressed Speech Is Correlated with the Ability to Listen in Modulated Noise | Journal of the Association for Research in Otolaryngology | Springer Nature Link","url":"https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10162-021-00832-0"},{"title":"Frontiers | Neural Oscillations Carry Speech Rhythm through to Comprehension","url":"https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/psychology/articles/10.3389/fpsyg.2012.00320/full"},{"title":"Word intelligibility decrements and the comprehension of time-compressed speech | Attention, Perception, & Psychophysics | Springer Nature Link","url":"https://link.springer.com/article/10.3758/BF03208200"},{"title":"The recognition of time-compressed speech as a function of age in listeners with cochlear implants or normal hearing","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC9557069/"},{"title":"Intelligibility of time-compressed synthetic speech: Compression method and speaking style - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639315000977"}]

Based on the search results, I found relevant research on the comprehension of compressed speech and the factors that limit it. Here are the key findings:

## Central Processing Limitations vs. Peripheral Factors

A reduced ability to perceive compressed speech—not related to any peripheral hearing deficits—is associated with advancing age and central auditory processing deficits. This directly supports the distinction you're asking about.

The ability to perceive fast speech is hypothesized to be limited by cortical processing mechanisms, with the decline in intelligibility at increasing speech rates resulting from a disruption in syllabic rhythm beyond the limits of what the cortical processing mechanisms can handle.

## Evidence Against Peripheral Intelligibility as the Primary Constraint

Research has shown that sensory limitations alone cannot explain the important drop in intelligibility already observed at 40% compression (93 ms/word) for both spoken and written sentences. Furthermore, intracranial recordings have documented "the ability of the core auditory cortex to follow the temporal speech envelope over a wide range of speaking rates," indicating that peripheral auditory processing is not the bottleneck.

The evidence suggests that comprehension of compressed speech is indeed more limited by central processing time constraints rather than by peripheral auditory intelligibility rates, as indicated by research showing central deficits can impair compressed speech perception independent of peripheral hearing sensitivity.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text:
I now have enough to evaluate. Let me assess what I've found.

**The source itself (Heiman, Leo, Leighbody & Bowler, 1986)** does directly conclude what the claim states. The article title is literally "Word intelligibility decrements and the comprehension of time-compressed speech," and the abstract/conclusion states "decreased comprehension of compressed messages is the result of decreased word intelligibility." So the claim is faithfully supported by the quote and source — this is checklist item 1 satisfied (no overreach in representing the source's own conclusion).

However, the claim is stated as a general scientific truth ("primarily caused by... not just by the faster delivery rate per se"), not merely "this 1986 paper argued." Under skeptical scrutiny, several problems emerge:

1. **The source is old (1986) and from a now-superseded paradigm.** Modern research (2012-2023) attributes the comprehension limit of compressed speech to **central/cortical processing constraints** — disruption of syllabic rhythm beyond what cortical neural oscillations can track, and a "temporal bottleneck in the language comprehension network" — NOT to peripheral word intelligibility per se. The PMC "Temporal Bottleneck" paper and the Nature/Frontiers neural-oscillation work directly frame the limit as central processing time, and explicitly note "sensory limitations alone cannot explain" the intelligibility drop.

2. **The claim's dichotomy is itself contested.** Word intelligibility and "delivery rate per se" are not cleanly separable — the dominant modern view is that *both* arise from a shared central timing/rhythm bottleneck, and that intelligibility decrements are themselves a *consequence* of rate exceeding cortical tracking limits. So saying the cause is word intelligibility "not just faster delivery rate" frames a 1986 finding as if it settles a question that subsequent literature reframed.

Let me verify the temporal-bottleneck framing to confirm the contradiction is real and credible.

input.prompt: Does this paper argue that comprehension of time-compressed speech is limited by a central processing/temporal bottleneck rather than by peripheral word intelligibility or by the acoustic faster delivery rate per se? What is the conclusion about what limits comprehension of accelerated speech? Quote the key conclusion.

content.content:
# Central Processing Bottleneck in Speech Comprehension

Yes, this paper argues that time-compressed speech comprehension is limited by a **central processing bottleneck** rather than peripheral factors.

## Key Conclusion

The authors state:

> "the main factor is not a sensory limitation but a central amodal processing bottleneck that leads to the overflow of an internal buffer"

## Supporting Evidence

The researchers found that:

1. **Sensory areas tracked stimulus duration linearly** - showing the auditory and visual systems could process fast input
2. **Higher language areas showed invariant responses** - left superior temporal sulcus and inferior frontal gyrus maintained constant activation across intelligible compression rates (60-100%), then suddenly collapsed
3. **Buffer saturation caused the breakdown** - when word presentation rate exceeded the brain's fixed integration speed (~250 ms per word), incoming words accumulated in a temporary storage buffer until retrieval capacity was exceeded

The findings demonstrate that language comprehension capacity is constrained by how quickly the brain can integrate words into meaningful sentences, not by how fast acoustic or visual signals can be delivered. This temporal constraint imposes a processing ceiling around 400 words per minute.

content.text:
This confirms a strong, credible, modern contradiction. Let me assess.

**Evaluation of the claim:**

The claim states comprehension reduction is "primarily caused by decreased intelligibility of individual words, not just by the faster delivery rate per se." This is a faithful restatement of the 1986 Heiman et al. source's own conclusion. So as a *report of what that paper found*, it's accurate.

But the claim is phrased as a general fact about the phenomenon, and the source quality/currency does not match that strength:

1. **Outdated (checklist item 4):** The source is from 1986. The field has moved substantially. The modern consensus (Vagharchakian et al. "Temporal Bottleneck," J. Neurosci., a high-quality fMRI/MEG study) directly states "the main factor is **not a sensory limitation** but a central amodal processing bottleneck." Word intelligibility per se is explicitly rejected as the primary limit. The Nature 2023, Frontiers 2012 neural-oscillation literature, and JARO 2021 work all converge on central/cortical syllabic-rhythm tracking limits — and explicitly note "sensory limitations alone cannot explain" the intelligibility drop seen even at 40% compression.

2. **Contradicted (checklist item 2):** The dominant modern framing contradicts the claim's causal attribution. The temporal-bottleneck account says comprehension collapses because central integration capacity (~250 ms/word) is exceeded — i.e., the limit IS fundamentally about delivery rate exceeding a central processing ceiling, the opposite of "not the faster delivery rate per se." Even the Heiman dichotomy is suspect because "word intelligibility" and "rate" are not cleanly separable; reduced intelligibility at high rates is itself a downstream consequence of rate.

3. The claim is also a *single-study* generalization. The 1986 study used only 500-word messages with interruption/backward-masking proxies (not actual extreme compression), at conditions like 60% signal deletion — methodologically dated proxies for the phenomenon.

The claim overgeneralizes a 39-year-old single study's conclusion into a settled causal fact, and the modern primary literature directly contradicts the "not the delivery rate per se" attribution by identifying a central processing-time/rate bottleneck as the primary limit. This is a refutation.

input.evidence:
The quote IS faithful to its source — Heiman, Leo, Leighbody & Bowler (1986), "Word intelligibility decrements and the comprehension of time-compressed speech" (Attention, Perception & Psychophysics / Perception & Psychophysics, DOI 10.3758/BF03208200) literally concludes "decreased comprehension of compressed messages is the result of decreased word intelligibility." So the claim accurately reports that one paper. The refutation is on source currency and contradiction, not misquotation.

(1) OUTDATED + SINGLE STUDY: The source is from 1986 and used dated proxies (two experiments on 500-word messages via temporal interruption and backward masking, e.g. 60% signal deletion) rather than actual extreme time compression. The claim elevates a 39-year-old single study's word-intelligibility account into a settled general causal fact ("primarily caused by... not just the faster delivery rate per se").

(2) CONTRADICTED BY MODERN PRIMARY LITERATURE: Vagharchakian et al., "A Temporal Bottleneck in the Language Comprehension Network" (J. Neurosci.; PMC6622343), an fMRI/MEG study, explicitly states "the main factor is NOT a sensory limitation but a central amodal processing bottleneck that leads to the overflow of an internal buffer." Sensory/word-level areas tracked stimulus duration linearly; comprehension collapsed when word rate exceeded a central integration speed (~250 ms/word, ceiling ~400 wpm). This directly inverts the claim: the limit IS rate exceeding a central processing-time ceiling, not word intelligibility per se. Converging modern work (Nature Sci. Reports 2023 s41598-023-29755-x; Frontiers Psychol. 2012 neural-oscillation syllabic-rhythm tracking; JARO 2021 PMC9085996) attributes the comprehension limit to central/cortical syllabic-rhythm tracking and notes "sensory limitations alone cannot explain" the intelligibility drop seen even at 40% compression.

(3) FALSE DICHOTOMY: "Word intelligibility" vs "delivery rate per se" are not cleanly separable; reduced intelligibility at high rates is itself a downstream consequence of rate exceeding cortical tracking limits, so the claim's causal split is itself contested by the dominant central-bottleneck account.
input.counterSource: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6622343/ (Vagharchakian et al., "A Temporal Bottleneck in the Language Comprehension Network," J. Neurosci.) — "the main factor is not a sensory limitation but a central amodal processing bottleneck"; corroborated by https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-023-29755-x and https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9085996/

content.text: Refutation submitted. Summary of finding: the claim faithfully reports its 1986 source (Heiman et al.), but is refuted on currency and contradiction grounds — it generalizes a single 39-year-old study into a settled causal fact, and the modern primary literature (Vagharchakian et al.'s "Temporal Bottleneck" J. Neurosci. study, plus converging neural-oscillation work) directly contradicts the "not the delivery rate per se" attribution by identifying a central processing-time/rate bottleneck — not peripheral word intelligibility — as the primary limit on comprehension of accelerated speech.
